key_facts.claim: Saccharomyces cerevisiae predominantly exists as diploid cells in nature, a life-cycle feature that limits classical genetic analyses of wild populations. Here, we establish a stable haploid strain collection derived from 33 diverse Taiwanese S. cerevisiae isolates through targeted disruption of the HO endonuclease gene. key_facts.claim: Improved and more accessible genome-sequencing approaches have allowed the analysis of large sets of natural yeast isolates. As a consequence, this unprecedented level of description of yeast-genome characteristics and variations in natural environments has provided crucial insights on yeast ecology and evolution.
key_facts.source: Going wild: ecology and genomics are crucial to understand yeast evolution - ScienceDirect

key_facts.claim: In 'Wild, Wild, Yeasts,' Melissa Petruzzello of Encyclopaedia Britannica discusses the ecology and uses of yeasts. She is joined by Michele Metych to talk about wild yeasts and sourdough bread-making. This is the thirteenth installment of the Botanize! audio series.
key_facts.source: Wild, Wild Yeasts | Botanize! Podcast and Transcript | Britannica

key_facts.claim: Oct 6, 2017 · Recently, the application of DNA-based culture-independent methods has taken an increasing role in the investigation of yeast biodiversity and ecology. In addition to the continuous discovery of novel yeast species, significant effort is being made to understand their function in their natural habitats.
